title: Beyond Variational Bias: Resolving Intertwined Orders in the Hubbard Model
authors: Luciano Loris Viteritti, Riccardo Rende, Christopher Roth, Anirvan Sengupta, Giuseppe Carleo, Antoine Georges
abstract: The two-dimensional Hubbard model at finite doping hosts competing or intertwined orders, resulting in conflicting conclusions from different computational approaches regarding its ground state. We show that a key source of such discrepancies is the bias encoded in the variational ansatz. We consider three different Transformer backflow fermionic wave functions based on a Slater determinant, its particle-hole counterpart, and a Pfaffian, initialized without any mean-field pretraining. We show that, despite achieving nearly degenerate, state-of-the-art variational energies, each ansatz converges to a state with qualitatively different spin, charge, and pairing correlations. Upon improving accuracy via symmetry restoration and variance reduction, however, all three converge to the same physical picture: coexisting superconducting and stripe orders. These results demonstrate that variational energy alone is insufficient to identify the ground state in the presence of competing phases, and highlight the importance of tracking how correlation functions evolve as the wave function is systematically improved before drawing physical conclusions.
summary: This paper addresses a critical issue in computational many-body physics: the tendency of different variational wave function architectures to favor different physical phases. By studying the 2D Hubbard model, the authors show that while different neural-network ansätze initially predict conflicting stripe or superconducting orders, applying symmetry restoration forces them to converge to a single, shared physical state. This demonstrates that energy minimization alone is insufficient to identify the ground state in systems with competing orders and highlights the necessity of tracking correlation functions during optimization.
main_problem: Investigating how architectural bias in different variational ansätze leads to conflicting physical conclusions regarding competing orders in the Hubbard model.
main_result: By using symmetry restoration and variance reduction, three different neural-network ansätze (determinant, particle-hole, and Pfaffian) converge to a consistent physical picture of coexisting superconducting and stripe orders.
method: Variational Monte Carlo using Transformer-based backflow fermionic wave functions, combined with symmetry projection (translational and rotational) and zero-variance extrapolation.
model_or_system: The two-dimensional t-t' Hubbard model on an 8x8 square lattice with periodic boundary conditions, specifically focusing on the regime with U=8, t'=-0.2, and 1/8 hole doping.
key_observables: d-wave superconducting order parameter (Delta_SC), stripe spin order parameter (M^2_str), spin structure factor S(k), and energy variance.
important_parameters: U = 8.0, t' = -0.2, hole doping delta = 1/8, 8x8 lattice size.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the true ground state can be recovered through systematic variance reduction and symmetry restoration, effectively overcoming the initial mean-field bias of the chosen ansatz.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the optimization landscape and convergence of different schemes; Figure 2 plots order parameters against energy variance; Figure 4 compares real-space charge/spin patterns; Figure 7 shows energy vs. variance for extrapolation; Figure 8 and 9 show the impact of symmetry restoration on pairing and spin structure factors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of variational bias, describes the Transformer-based backflow architecture and the three antisymmetrization schemes, presents results showing how unprojected states diverge, demonstrates convergence through symmetry restoration, and concludes with the physical implications for intertwined orders.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the optimization land- scape for different antisymmetrization schemes. Contours represent variational energy levels. Starting from random initialization and without symmetry constraints, determi- nant, determinant–particle-hole, and Pfaffian constructions converge toward qualitatively distinct solutions with nearly degenerate energies but different dominant orders. Upon vari- ational energy reduction and symmetry restoration, all tra- jectories approach the same correlated ground state in which superconducting and stripe order coexist.

FIG. 2. Superconducting and stripe order parameters as a function of variational accuracy for the 8×8 Hubbard model under PBC at U = 8.0, t′ = −0.2, and δ = 1/8 [see Eq. (1)]. Panel a: d-wave pairing order parameter ∆SC versus energy variance per site. Panel b: Stripe spin order parameter M 2 str versus variance per site. The definitions of the order parameters are given in the main text. In panels (a) and (b), different colors correspond to the three antisymmetrization schemes: determinant (red), determinant in particle hole (PH) (blue) and Pfaffian (orange). Different marker symbols indicate the degree of symmetry restoration without symmetry projections: no symmetry projection (circles),...

FIG. 3. Absolute value of the d-wave pairing correlations |Cp(r)| [see Eq. (2)] as a function of distance |r| on an 8 × 8 square lattice with PBC at U = 8.0, t′ = −0.2, and δ = 1/8. Panel a. Results without symmetry projection. Panel b. Results after restoring translational and rotational symme- tries.

Figure 3 reports the absolute value of the pairing cor- relation functions |Cp(r)| [see Eq. (2)] as a function of the distance |r| in the lattice for the three antisymmetriza- tion schemes. In panel (a), we report the results be- fore symmetry projections. The determinant backflow ansatz exhibits a pronounced suppression of long-range pairing correlations. For |r| ≳3, the signal is nearly one order of magnitude smaller with the respect to the other states. The determinant-PH backflow state alle- viates this suppression, providing a solution closer to the Pfaffian ansatz. Enforcing the symmetries, along- side the quantitative improvement in energy and vari- ance (see Table I), we observe a...

FIG. 4. Real-space charge density and spin patterns for the 8 × 8 Hubbard model with PBC at U = 8.0, t′ = −0.2, and δ = 1/8 [see Eq. (1)]. Columns compare the three variational constructions: determinant, Pfaffian, and determinant in the PH representation. The top row shows results without symmetry projection, while the bottom row shows the corresponding symmetry-projected results. Panels a,b,c: Charge density N(r) without symmetry projection. Panels d,e,f: Local spin ⟨ˆSz r⟩ without symmetry projection. Panels g,h,i: Density-density correlations CD(r) after symmetry projection. Panels l,m,n: Spin-spin correlations CS(r) after symmetry projection. Below each panel we show the...

FIG. 5. s-wave pairing correlation function Cs-wave(r) [see Eq. (4)] for the attractive Hubbard model with U = −8.0, t′ = 0.0, and δ = 1/8 on an 8 × 8 lattice along the diagonal of the square. Results are shown for determinant, determinant in PH and Pfaffian, symmetry-restored determinant ansatz, and DQMC.

FIG. 6. In the Transformer Backflow Architecture a physi- cal configuration s (see definition in the main text) is mapped through an Embedding layer into a sequence of vectors, which are then processed by a stack of nl Transformer layers. The Transformer output is fed into a Fermionic Output Layer that constructs configuration-dependent orbitals. These orbitals, together with the fermionic occupation numbers n, are com- bined via the chosen antisymmetrization scheme (determinant or Pfaffian) to produce the wavefunction amplitude Ψθ(s).

FIG. 8. Real-space pairing-pairing correlation function [see Eq. (2)] for the 8 × 8 Hubbard model at U = 8.0, t′ = −0.2, and doping δ = 1/8, obtained with the determinant ansatz before [panel (a)] and after [panel (b)] symmetry restora- tion. Below each panel we show the one-dimensional cut Cp(x, y = 3).

FIG. 7. Variational energy per site as a function of the energy variance per site for the 8 × 8 Hubbard model with periodic boundary conditions at U = 8.0, t′ = −0.2, and dop- ing δ = 1/8. Results are shown for the Pfaffian state with different levels of accuracy obtained without symmetry pro- jection (circles), with translational symmetry (rhombi), and with both translational and rotational symmetries (triangles) restored. A linear fit of the data is used to extrapolate to zero variance and estimate the ground-state energy (red star).

FIG. 9. Spin structure factor for the 8 × 8 Hubbard model at U = 8.0, t′ = −0.2, and doping δ = 1/8, obtained with the Pfaffian ansatz at different levels of symmetry restoration. Panel a: Result without symmetry projection. Panel b: Result after restoring translational symmetry. Panel c: Result after restoring both translational and rotational symmetries.
